translations.verbatim_quote: O mighty-armed Kṛṣṇa, does not such a man, who is bewildered from the path of transcendence, fall away from both spiritual and material success and perish like a riven cloud, with no position in any sphere?

O mighty-armed Krishna, does not such a man, who is bewildered from the path of transcendence, fall away from both spiritual and material success and perish like a riven cloud, with no position in any sphere?

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (fair-use excerpt; full purport length: 1377 chars)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: There are two ways to progress. Those who are materialists have no interest in transcendence; therefore they are more interested in material advancement by economic development, or in promotion to the higher planets by appropriate work.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 6.38 Mahabaho, O Mighty-armed one; ubhaya-vibhrastah, fallen from both, having fallen from the Path of Action and the Path of Yoga; apratisthah, without support; vimudhah, deluded-having become deluded; brahmanah pathi, on the path of Brahman, on the path leading to Brahman; kaccit na, doe

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 6.37 – 6.39 Arjuna said — What way does he go, who has embarked on Yoga endowed with faith, but who by inadequacy of exertion in practice, does not gain success in Yoga and has his mind wandering from Yoga? Does he not perish like a small piece of cloud torn from a large mass of cloud — pe
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-6-38-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Ramanuja of Sri Sampradaya:)

positions.rendering: deviated from both — both spiritual and material success
fallen from karma-mārga (abandoned for yoga) and from yoga-mārga (yoga not completed)
doubly fallen — from the practice attempted and from the worldly conduct given up

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: All three readings agree: 'both' refers to the loss of the worldly position (given up for the practice) AND the loss of the practice itself (not completed). The simile of the torn cloud names the fear: dissipation without arrival.

literal_meaning: Surely, O mighty-armed, fallen from both, having no support, deluded on the path of brahman — does he not perish like a torn cloud?
traditional_meaning_consensus: 6.38 sharpens the fear. The fallen yogi is ubhaya-vibhraṣṭa — fallen from both: from worldly attainment (because he renounced it for the practice) AND from yogic attainment (because he failed to complete the practice). The simile chinnābhram iva — like a cloud severed from the larger cloud-mass, drifting and dissolving — is precise: the cloud has lost its source (the cloud-mass) and has not yet reached its destination (becoming rain), and so simply dissipates. Apratiṣṭha — without support, without firm ground. Vimūḍha — deluded — on the path of brahman. Sankara's reading: he is fallen both from karma-mārga (because he abandoned it for yoga) and from yoga-mārga (because he did not complete yoga); like a cloud cut off, he perishes. Ramanuja's reading: he is doubly-fallen — from the practice he was attempting AND from the worldly conduct he had given up. Both traditions read this as the most distressed form of the question. The simile is famous and is itself the answer the next teaching will refute in 6.40.
